planting.note: Divide rhizomes or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; African wild iris is remarkably drought-tolerant once established but benefits from regular watering during the first growing season — do not cut the flower stalks after blooming, as the same stalks rebloom for multiple years, and removing them sacrifices future flowering.

directions: Plant rhizomes or container specimens in well-drained, average to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; African wild iris is a South African rhizomatous perennial of the iris family (Iridaceae) that is one of the most reliable, long-lived, and drought-tolerant evergreen perennials for Gulf Coast and warm-climate gardens, producing large, white, six-petaled flowers 3–4 inches across with bright-yellow and blue-violet marks at the petal bases on tall, branching, flat, fan-like stems from spring through early summer and intermittently at other seasons. The plant's most unusual feature is that it reblooms on the same persistent flower stems for multiple years — so the stems should never be removed after blooming, as the following season's flowers emerge from the same stalk. The fans of sword-shaped, dark-green, evergreen leaves are ornamental year-round and the plant spreads slowly into large, weed-suppressing clumps. Extremely adaptable to heat, drought, alkaline soil, and coastal conditions; an outstanding low-maintenance evergreen perennial for zone 8–11 public and residential landscapes, often seen lining roads and medians throughout the Gulf South.

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; blue stars self-seeds freely in suitable climates and can spread into a loose, naturalistic colony — deadhead after the main flush to reduce self-seeding if self-propagation is not desired in formal garden settings.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in partial shade to full sun; blue stars is a South African evergreen perennial of the iris family (Iridaceae) that produces loose, branching stems bearing numerous small, intensely bright, sky-blue to violet-blue, six-petaled, star-shaped flowers — each individual flower is short-lived (opening in the morning and closing by afternoon) but the plant produces a continuous succession of fresh blooms throughout the warm season, creating a sustained display of clear blue that is one of the few true blues available in warm-climate gardens. The narrow, grass-like, dark-green, evergreen leaves form a compact, fine-textured, clump-forming basal rosette from which the flowering stems arise. Blue stars performs acceptably in partial shade conditions, which distinguishes it from many other blue-flowered perennials that require full sun. It self-seeds freely in moist climates and can naturalize into loose colonies in suitable conditions. An underused and valuable source of true-blue flower color for warm-climate shade and partial-shade gardens in zone 9–11.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring with at least one growing bud per division, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; scarlet ginger is among the hardier hedychiums and establishes more readily than many tender species — rhizomes benefit from mulching in zone 7 gardens to protect against hard freezes, where the tops die back but established rhizomes often persist and resprout.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; scarlet ginger (orange ginger) is a Himalayan species widely recognized as producing some of the most elegantly narrow and intensely fragrant of all hedychium flower spikes — in summer and early fall, dense, 8–12-inch, cylindrical spikes emerge from the tips of the leafy pseudostems and open a succession of narrow-petaled flowers in vivid orange-red to bright scarlet, each with projecting, vivid-red stamens and filaments, creating a spike of spidery, intensely colored, flame-like flowers. The fragrance is deeply sweet, penetrating, and somewhat different from other hedychiums — more spicy and persistent. Scarlet ginger is among the cold-hardiest of the ornamental hedychiums (to approximately 5–10°F in zone 7 when rhizomes are mulched) and one of the most drought-tolerant after establishment. The tall, upright pseudostems clothed in glossy, lance-shaped leaves create an architectural tropical effect in the partial-shade border. An excellent and relatively hardy Himalayan ginger for zone 7–10 gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall (September–November in zone 8–9) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; watsonia corms planted in fall establish roots before winter and bloom the following spring. In zone 9 Gulf South they naturalize readily and can be left in the ground year-round in well-drained sites.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in fall in well-drained, average to sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; watsonia is a South African cormous perennial of the iris family that produces tall, elegant, narrowly branching spikes of funnel-shaped, soft-pink to bright-pink or lavender-pink flowers in spring, carried on sturdy, 3–5-foot stems above a fan-like base of narrow, sword-shaped, bright-green leaves resembling those of a large gladiolus. The individual flowers are attractive and open sequentially up the spike over several weeks. Watsonia has naturalized extensively in parts of coastal Australia and in suitable Gulf South areas and can form large, self-perpetuating colonies in well-drained soil — the corms persist and multiply year to year without lifting, making it more reliably perennial than gladiolus in zone 8–10. After flowering the foliage dies back in summer, and the plants rest through the heat before resuming growth in fall. An excellent and easily naturalized spring-blooming ornamental and cut flower for Gulf Coast mild-winter gardens.

planting.note: Divide corm chains in early spring before growth resumes, or plant container specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; giant montbretia establishes quickly and multiplies vigorously by corm offsets — divide every 3–4 years when clumps become crowded, as congested corms produce fewer and shorter flower stems.

directions: Divide corm chains or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; giant montbretia is the largest and boldest of the garden-grown crocosmia species — it produces much wider, strongly pleated, bright-green, sword-like, upward-arching leaves (considerably broader than those of common C. × crocosmiiflora hybrids) and in summer, tall, 3–4-foot arching stems carry dense, one-sided racemes of notably large, widely flared, horizontal, deep-orange to flame-orange flowers. Unlike the common montbretia hybrids whose flowers hang pendant or downward along the raceme, Crocosmia masoniorum holds its flowers horizontally or upward-facing, giving the raceme a more open and showy display effect with the flowers projecting prominently outward from the stem. The species is the parent of several important crocosmia cultivars, including the popular 'Lucifer'. It is less invasive than the common C. × crocosmiiflora forms that can spread aggressively. An excellent, bold, and architecturally striking perennial for summer color, cut flowers, and partial-shade borders in zone 6–9.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
